comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is  here, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions involving the evocation of spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my thirty-eighth published book and my fourth published novel; it's also a book I had no intention of writing, and it's one of my personal favorites of my novels. Beginning around the time that I finished the first draft of A World Full of Gods, my exploration of polytheism, I started mulling over the prospect of saying the same things in fiction using the Great Old Ones, H.P. Lovecraft's tentacled monster-gods, as a way to talk about our culture's fear and hatred of anything superhuman and transcendent. I sketched out a few bits of rough draft here and there, but never did much with it.

Then over the course of a few days in the fall of 2015 an entire novel downloaded itself into my brain and would not leave me any peace until I wrote it. I hammered out the 70,000-word first draft in eight weeks -- the fastest I've ever written anything on that scale. The result was a lively tentacular romp in which Owen Merrill, a grad student at Miskatonic University in Arkham, Massachusetts, finds out that H.P. Lovecraft's "fictional" creations are anything but imaginary, that everything he knows about history is wrong, and that those writhing tentacles may hold the last hope for the survival of the earth. There are sequels, but it stands alone tolerably well as a novel. If you're interested, you can get a copy here if you live in the United States and here if you live elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: My Take on an Intro to the Planes and Question on Astral Production vs Perception
comments.content:
Good Evening,

For anyone who wants it, a very happy beginning to August!

As for the book, I was late to the party with The Weird of Hali, reading the main series for the first time from the end of last year through the beginning of this year, and I have the side-books to get to soon, but I thoroughly enjoyed them, despite (because of?) years of reading and roleplaying with Lovecraftian baddies. Thanks very much for your enjoyably unorthodox take!

To Share: As much to solidify my own understanding as anything else, I've put together a brief primer on the seven planes of being as taught in JMG's work, such as the Occult Philosophy Workbook: https://jpowellrussell.com/#a_beginner_s_manual_of_the_planes

To Ask: In putting together the above summary, I found myself grappling the most with the Astral Plane, perhaps unsurprisingly given its focus in the spiritual work I'm doing. One concept I'm still working through is how much of astral experience is creation versus how much perception - in other words, how much of our imaginal life tends to be "made up" by each of us, and how much is "found" somewhere "out there"? Besides being a fine theme for ongoing meditation, any thoughts on what the default is for this and how much spiritual practice alters it?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: My Take on an Intro to the Planes and Question on Astral Production vs Perception
replies.content:
1) Thank you! The Weird of Hali seems to be slithering out through the crawlspaces of contemporary culture at its own pace, finding those people who are attracted to its squamous and rugose qualities. It's all very reminiscent of Lovecraft's story "The Call of Cthulhu"...

2) It is indeed a fine theme for meditation! All astral experience is co-created and involves an intricate web of feedback loops, so that it's rarely if ever possible to identify any astral content as either entirely made or entirely found. The great difference to watch is actually not a matter of inside vs. outside, made vs. found, but a question of which levels of self and other are involved. In the ordinary, uninitiated person, the subconscious mind and popular culture form the two poles between which the astral patterns are woven. In the initiate, the superconscious mind and the transcultural, transcendent aspects of outside reality provide different poles.

Trees in particular benefit from emotional interactions with beings who have astral bodies, since they're in the process of evolving astral sheaths into astral bodies themselves. Plants generally benefit from positive energy, but with trees, it's crucial. Human beings, however, play only a small part in that work. Most of it's done by birds, who are basically tree angels -- bright luminous balls of emotion and intelligence, radiating on the astral plane, who easily perform what to trees is the supreme miracle: moving from place to place at will.

That said, human beings can certainly help, because any interaction trees have with a being with an astral body has an inductive effect. In terms of other plants, our affection and care is simply a general blessing to them, though here again we're playing second fiddle to bees and other insects, who adore them and help them enrich and strengthen their etheric bodies. Have you ever noticed that the more bees visit a flowering plant, the stronger the scent of its flowers becomes?

comments.title: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale
comments.content:
Last week's discussion of reincarnation (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/289713.html?thread=50145201#cmt50145201) was quite an eye opener for me. I had believed that humans went through incarnations for a longer period of time than 2000-ish years.

What really gave me my Owen-Merill-in-the-parking-garage moment, though, was your comment that "Once we're born into the discarnate state, we begin our real lives, and we have much, much more to learn thereafter."

You have spoken of deep time and I understood it intellectually, but now it hit me in the gut-- all this work as a human will take "only" 2,000-ish years, with a gigantic span of more time ahead, and a gigantic amount of more work and growing to do. And how gigantic of a span have I already spent as other creatures, plants etc before this? To >see< this expanse of time in my mind was boggling, and rather overwhelming. I retreated into a session of listening to "Blade Runner Blues", and wept... from an emotion that I'm not sure has a name. But during the song the gentle comment came to me: "there will be beauty, too."

[IMAGE:0]

Fruit flies have been much on my mind lately. As it turns out, they are a great learning metaphor. "As below, so above"-- as much larger as we are than fruit flies, so there are other beings that much larger than us. As Athaia pointed out in that previous thread, many of these larger beings are "less than benevolent".

We certainly are hard on fruit flies. In addition to all the efforts to kill them when they enter our houses, we do things to them "in the name of science" such as forcing them to fight each other (https://www.kqed.org/science/1494923/these-fighting-fruit-flies-are-superheroes-of-brain-science), cutting off a leg to see whether they feel chronic pain (they do) (https://www.smithsonianmag.com/smart-news/insects-can-experience-chronic-pain-study-finds-180972656/); and a browse through the major scientific supply catalogs reveals greater horrors for them. If the larger beings regard us, and treat us, the way we treat fruit flies, no wonder our lives are often hard!

Then I pondered all of this, both the "reincarnation road" and what comes after it, having been created by, and being administered by, a Being who is larger than all of it, and thus mind-bogglingly huge. Of Whom nevertheless it is said, "what is man that you are mindful of him?"... and He is, I've experienced it personally.

If we are as fruit flies to the higher beings, and we pray, then, I wonder, do insects also pray? Consider Wood Ant Colony Two, trapped in the bottom of a Soviet-era bunker (https://www.atlasobscura.com/articles/cannibal-ants-soviet-nuclear-bunker). With no way out and no hope for any future other than slow starvation, they nevertheless maintained their ant civilization, while a team of scientists watched. Did they pray for deliverance? The scientists' readers demanded that they help the ants, and deliverance arrived-- in the form of a very long 2x4 board leading to the sunlit world above.

I once accidentally rescued a beetle from a spiderweb. I'd planned to clean off the webbing and add the beetle to my insect collection-- but when I removed the spiderweb mummy wrappings the beetle turned out to be alive and well. I have never since seen so much joy emanate from something so small, as from that beetle standing on my fingertip, redeemed from certain death.

Would it not behoove us to behave like more benevolent "gods" to our smaller fellow creatures? Perhaps this is how the concept of 'ahimsa' came to be.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale
replies.content:
I know the feeling. It really is stunning to realize both how very small each individual life of ours is in the great scheme of things, and how vast a trajectory we trace out over the whole life of each individual soul.

I hadn't heard about the wood ant colony. I bet their descendants for ten thousand generations will remember how a group of distant gods pressured the gods who ruled over them to grant them the miracle of the two-by-four in response to their prayer. That beetle's descendants have a similar story to tell.

If I may pass on a story of my own, I've made it a habit for many years, when a moth or some other insect gets trapped inside my apartment, to catch it once it alights (I use a clear plastic rice measure and a thin piece of cardboard) and put it out the window. Word has apparently spread among insect-kind; these days, when a moth gets into the house, quite often it will fly over to the desk where I'm sitting, land someplace close by, flutter up into the rice measure when I put that over it, and go joyfully out into the night once I take it to the window and open the screen. They're much less stupid than we think.

replies.title: Re: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale
replies.content:
Moths and rice measures: That is so seriously awesome!!!

Once many years ago my boss spotted me catching a spider on the manufacturing plant floor. The technique (which I also use for flies on the windowpane) is to take a loosely-crumpled wad of tissue paper and trap the small creature in its folds. (Diligent and careful chasing is often needed in order to accomplish this.) The paper does the restraining, so I don't squash the poor wee beastie with my fingertips. I take the wad of tissue outside, open it up, and the spider or fly flees for cover, unharmed.

Some time after I explained this to him, he said to me that it had made such an impression on him, that now he was doing that too!

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale
replies.content:
Another anecdote to add to the TSW pile...

My family makes it a habit to catch and safely release (nearby but away from our home and children's play areas) all sorts of critters, including, much to others' horror, brown recluses, scorpions, and venomous snakes like copperheads.

We've since had multiple moments where a dangerous creature announced it's presence just in the nick of time: we discovered a baby rattlesnake coming into our house, a brown recluse crawled out from under a pillow right in front of us, and multiple scorpions have moved in view just before we would have invaded their space. Each time, we were able to barely avoid a potentially calamitous situation.

The first couple times I was shocked and relieved, but the consistency and repetition have convinced me there's a connection and some sort of communication going on that respects our mutual desire to live and let live.

replies.title: Re: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale
replies.content:
Along these lines, Ursula LeGuin wrote a very moving, very short story called 'Mazes,', included in her book "Buffalo Gals". It's about a being, by implication a lab mouse or rat, being tortured by a researcher running it through mazes hundreds of times. The mouse tries every means in its repertoire to communicate its exhaustion and desperation to the alien being who's trying to control it, including finally dancing the most exquisite, most secret and sublime dances of its tribe. Throughout the story the mouse is trying to figure out whether the researcher is a sentient being capable of communication, and tragically for both, concludes that the researcher is not. All of the stories in this book follow similar themes.

This also makes me think of the mice who are actually running the galaxy in Douglas Adam's "Hitchhiker" series, who bring about life on Earth to serve as a living computer whose purpose is to discover the meaning of life. (The answer our 'computer' comes up with is the famous "42.")

A lot of people think about these things...and it has made some difference in our behavior toward non-humans, I guess, but not enough. I suppose this is not too surprising when we see how members of our own species treat each other, all too often.

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale

replies.title: Re: Pondering Reincarnation And Differences of Scale
replies.content:
Thank you for sharing your thoughts! I think a lot depends on our position in relation to insect or plant life. We usually think (and frequently are) in a position of power which enables us to decide between being a cruel or benevolent "god". And it certainly makes me sad that so many people opt to being cruel. On the other hand, this position of relative power is the result of a very long process - it would possibly be fair to call it a fight.

For some time, we tried to cultivate two vegetable gardens in two different places. The first was formerly mowed green land and it was pretty easy to turn it into a vegetable garden which is both productive and bursting with flowers and insect life, even for us newbies. The second spot was a former vegetable garden which was given up decades ago and turned into wilderness thereafter. We cleared it again and tried for three consecutive years to turn it productive. And let me tell you this - the pressure of the surrounding wildlife, all kinds of critters and plants, was so intense that it turned out to be almost impossible for us. We could have played the cruel god, and if we had invested a lot of energy, if we had hit very hard and slashed the wildlife back in a certain not too small area around the garden we might have had succeeded - but for us it wasn't worth the price and we decided to invest our energy in the other garden, which was a very good decision.

And then the chicken - foxes are pressing very hard this year and many chicken keepers lost a few of their animals. Last week we lost 9 out of 11 and the remaining chicken died two days later of their injuries. After they died I decided not to bury them but I laid them openly in the chicken enclosure so that the fox might get them, too. And in the late evening, he surely came and took his tribute. But in harder times, carelessness and defeat like this would be very problematic and we have to make sure that a failure like this doesn't repeat.

When pondering examples like these, it feels like it is only a very short walk between being on the "god" end of the pole or on the other. And what stories like those you have shared here are telling me again and again is that we as a society are wasting precious time and energy in playing god while we should be (re-)learning to dance on the pole with elegance - which will quite likely involve the whole spectrum from benevolence to being hard and possibly even cruel - but quite differently to the cruelty the laboratory-gods are displaying, too. We have lost almost any measure and balance - and judging from the little experience we gathered with our small vegetable garden and livestock this might be a cause for great trouble in the future, because it takes very long and painful failures to regain the balance.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I am very confused at the moment. I got married two weeks ago and Cupid was very generous and had two arrows in his quiver for me. I fell in love with another woman at the wedding (now you and the commentariat are welcome to laugh. It's one of the many curious episodes that run through my whole life, it seems to be my horoscope). I knew this person before, but when I saw her in the pretty dress and beautiful make-up, it happened. I've never liked a person so much on the outside. At least that's what I imagine.

Although I'm currently on my honeymoon and I love my wife more than anything, I can only think about her and have the usual symptoms of being in love. She is now the center of my universe and I associate everything that comes up in my everyday life with her. I fantasize that we could still get together and all that bullshit. It's fucking weird because I've only exchanged a few sentences with this person in my life and my wife should actually be at the center of my thoughts now.

The whole thing is so awkward because she is also a subordinate of my wife's at work and she herself is taken. Also, my wife and I recently bought a house. It feels like I've finished a jigsaw puzzle and then realized that one piece fits much better. But if I replace that piece, a nuclear bomb goes off. in short, going for that feeling is absolutely not an option.

In our society, being in love is often portrayed as sacred. I have of course been in love many times in my life, but now after a few years of spiritual practice as a Buddhist, it feels like a mental illness to me...

I have two questions:

1) I am dealing with the subject of infatuation because of this story. What does occultism have to say about this topic? I've read on the internet that being in love with a person indicates previous karmic connections or soul contracts made before birth. But that could be some new age nonsense.

2) Do you have any tips on how to let go of the feeling of infatuation quickly? In my tradition it is recommended to focus on the hideousness of the human body, however I am afraid this will reflect on my wife.

Thank you very much!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
0) Before we go on to your questions, may I make a suggestion? You really need to figure out why you're trying to sabotage your marriage. I recommend sitting down with a notebook and a pen, writing "Why I hate being married" at the top of the page, and then just start writing down whatever comes to mind, without editing or censoring anything. In subsequent days, vary the question to include things such as "Why I hate my wife" and "Why I want to ruin my life." This kind of half-conscious self-sabotage is very common, it causes a vast amount of entirely preventable human misery, and you can stop doing it any time you want to.

1) Infatuation is a form of projection, to use Carl Jung's terminology. You take an archetype -- one of the basic mental categories we all carry in our heads -- and project it onto someone else. In your case, it's the archetype Jung called the anima, basically the image of the Perfect Woman, and it functions in the same way as beer goggles:
[IMAGE:0]
Now and again there can be past life issues involved; for example, you may be being given a chance to make the same mistake you made in previous lives. You don't have to fall for it again, you know.

2) You can deal with it by realizing, first, that you're doing it to yourself; second, that there are reasons why you're doing it, and you're probably not facing those squarely; and third, that once you face up to those reasons, it will stop.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you JMG for your honest words, I will take them to heart.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Make sure those working notes are in no location where your wife can find them. Those also would be thermonuclear... =8O

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
2) Another aid in quickly getting to the bottom of an unwanted and self-destructive habit is to consider what bindings, ensorcelments, obsessions, curses, etc. may have been affixed to you by others, in order to further their own agendas regardless of whatever impact that would have on you. Most of our deep, inexplicable patterns get laid down very early on, so reviewing parental and sibling relationships can be very useful, especially based on the sudden, full-blown, ill-timed nature of your obsession — there's some deep stuff buried in there! Who really doesn't want you to get married, break away, succeed, individuate, etc.? Did you have a jealous sibling who always resented your succeeding where he or she hadn't? Did you have an overly-entwined parent who chafed every time you tried to separate?

Then there's your wife's family, potentially bringing their own hopes for your marriage to fail, or even your wife herself, aiming to stay in some uncomfortable comfort zone by being disappointed. There's also the siren who has captivated your attention; perhaps that's an unwanted gift she nonchalantly tosses into the middle of every wedding she attends. Add to that exes, bosses, jealous drinking buddies, etc., and you'll have a long list of who might be over-charging a distracting habit within you to turn it into a full-blown obsession. The habit is yours, but it may have all sorts of binding parasites clinging to it, amping it up beyond anything you're used to having to deal with. Whatever is coming from you and whatever is coming from others can both get decoded through journaling (dream journaling can also be a great way to catch intuitions bubbling up from your soul.)

Identifying others' contribution to your obsession isn't at all about getting off the hook or blaming others; it's about figuring out who is pouring energy into your life to produce outcomes diametrically opposed to your own interests. Once you realize who slimed you, however long ago, dismantling whatever they put on you becomes a heck of a lot easier. Then you can work on your own divided will, fear of commitment, and sabotaging habits, without having to constantly battle against others' willful agendas. How much do you personally not want to commit and flourish, and how much do others not want you to be able to commit and flourish. It's very much worth knowing how that ratio divides out.

And don't forget, you're now in a partnership, so you don't have to resolve this all on your own. That may take some getting used to, and may be more than a little bit scary, but that is the real gift that marriage has to offer.

— Christophe

replies.content:
Actually, many cultures do regard romantic love as a form of madness--one reason that they don't regard it as a basis for marriage and family. Consider Paris in the Trojan War myths. Ordinarily he would be a complete villain, having betrayed the sacred bonds of hospitality by stealing his host's wife. But he is partially off the hook because he is being manipulated by Aphrodite--and the same is true for Helen--they are both victims of forces larger than themselves, not merely seducer and adulteress. There are reasons that Hera, not Aphrodite, rules marriage.

Rita

comments.title: Mind-to-mind contact: by what medium?
comments.content:
When you discover that your strongly felt thoughts have somehow propagated and been picked up by another person, on what plane or planes does this thought reach them? Is it by the astral light, etheric force, or some combination?

This has happened to me twice in the last few years. On one occasion the influence of a pharmaceutical product was involved; it rendered me more sensitive to another person’s awareness, and I subsequently learned that my impression was correct.

On the more recent occasion my idea was conveyed by thought alone, and the reality of mind-to-mind communication was demonstrated to me in an amusing yet absolutely unmistakable way. Ever since this incident I’ve known for a fact that telepathy is real, whereas before I merely speculated about it.

Also, I recollect many years ago having a distinct premonition about something significant that turned out to be true.

When we have experiences like this, what exactly are we doing? On what plane do such impressions travel?

replies.title: Re: Mind-to-mind contact: by what medium?
replies.content: That's an astral phenomenon. The astral plane is the plane of emotions, imagination, and ordinary thoughts, and you broadcast on that plane at all times; so does everyone else. This kind of astral perception is very common -- it's just that our culture insists that it can't happen and treats any instance when it does happen like a loud public fart. Regular meditation will help you attune to the astral plane, and make such experiences more common -- astral telepathy, I mean, not public farting. ;-)

Welcome JMG and the Magic Monday community

I wish you all a good week and every development and success.
Today I have questions from quite a broad spectrum, as here are questions from me and a friend of mine who asked me to ask them.

1. does anyone know of any books/traditions similar to the Franz Bardon tradition in the aspect of , "energy-based" where the main tool is , "etheric courrents". And a question to JMG strikte, does the Franz Bardon tradition count as a system of magical development (where we define magic using the definition used in the Magic Monday FAQ, i.e. the Dion Fortune definition)?

3. in your book , "monsters" you wrote quote: while its misleading to think of etheric level as some sort of , "other dimention" in science-fiction terms. It surrounds us constantly and can be perceived directly by anyone willing to learn and practice relatively simple exercises. What are these exercises or in which book can they be found?

And my friend's questions.
A brief description, he lives in a place surrounded by gigantic tracts of forest. Not far from the Whitetower forest (the last ancient primeval forest in Europe, east side of Poland). Where he observes a fair amount of activity which, as he describes, is very reminiscent of fairy activity. My friend is a foreign, so he has no one in his family he could ask about folk practices, and the locals are reportedly not too keen to share such knowledge with someone "outside the community".

So his questions:
1. In your book ,,monsters'' you described that there are magical techniques and activities to build a good relationship with fairies. These fairies are not aggressive they just walk in the forest and sometimes they come near my windows. Sometimes I also hear knocking inside the house.

I would like to build a good relationship with my 'ethereal neighbours'. After all, we live on the same land. I recently went out to clean up in a nearby forest, will this be an activity pleasing to the faeries?

2.I have returned to physical training and am wondering if there are any schools or courses or books that combine occult techniques and physical training? Something along the lines of , 'invoke within yourself the powers of ancient body builders and lift weights to develop your spirit and body' ?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'll label your questions A and your friend's B.

A-1) Bardon's system is certainly a method of magical development but it includes much more etheric practice than most systems in the English-speaking world. There's plenty of other material on the same sort of thing in central European languages, but very little has been turned into English so I can't claim to know it well.

A-2) It depends on the poetry. Some poetry focuses on emotional states, in which case it enriches the astral body. Some focuses on meaning, in which case it helps build the mental sheath. The very best poetry does both.

A-3) There are literally thousands of systems and at least as many books. It depends on what kind of work with the life force you want to do.

B-1) Find out what the local customs were. Faeries aren't all the same all over the world; like humans, they have regional and local cultures. Find out how the local people in your area maintained good relations with the neighboring spirits, and do those things; the local Leszy, Rusalki, and Pojawki will appreciate it.

B-2) There used to be such things in the Western tradition but those, along with so much else, got suppressed by the materialist dogmas of the age of reason. One old practice I know of, though, is to establish a relationship with the spirit of one of the great bodybuilders of the past. If there's one you particularly admire, get a good picture of him doing a classic lift, put it in your home gym, and ask his spirit to inspire and guide you as you seek to emulate his feats.

replies.title: (no subject)

On 1), one thing to try might be to pause at any particularly emotional scene in the next novel you read and stop and imagine yourself in that situation, focusing especially on how it would make you feel. Another, possibly silly suggestion, might be to read some young adult or romance novels - such books both tend to focus more on the emotional states of the characters than some other genres and also to spell them out rather than implying them (Hemmingway would be terrible for this - all of the emotions in his books are implied for the reader to infer).

On the young adult front, I can recommend Six of Crows, which is a fantasy heist story revolving around a crew from not-Amsterdam raiding a magical not-Nordic super-fortress. I read it for the heisty stuff, but how the characters feel (especially about the other characters!) is quite the focus for much of it.

Cheers,
Jeff

Schizophrenia and meditation

Hello JMG and everyone else, I have a question. I'm schizophrenic and as such have problems with disorganised thinking which affects my ability to meditate quite heavily. I sit there and immediately my mind begins to wander and most of the time I repeat a song over and over (these last few weeks it's been Puff the Magic Dragon) and I constantly try to bring my mind back to focusing on my breathing but it literally lasts just a few seconds then it's back to the song... does anyone have experience with schizophrenia and meditation who could give me some advice on what to do to help with meditation, specifically how to focus?

Thanks
MWT

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia and meditation
replies.content:
Let me start by reassuring you. People who have the most un-schizophrenic minds around have exactly the same trouble you're having when they start learning to meditate. Yes, including the music! I still sometimes have to make a special effort not to have some piece of music I heard recently interrupt my meditations.

That is to say, what you're experiencing is absolutely normal. The vast majority of the people I've taught how to meditate have had exactly the same experiences you're having now, and I had them, too, when I was first learning how to meditate. By bringing your mind back to focusing on your breathing, over and over again, you're doing exactly the right thing.

Mental focus is like a muscle, but it's a muscle that most people never use at all, so it's really weak. Just keep exercising it. A few seconds of concentration at a time is a positive gain. I know it won't feel like much, but if you keep practicing every day, bit by bit you'll find your capacity to focus improving. Step by step, as the Taoists say, we walk the thousand-mile road.

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia and meditation
replies.content:
Pop ditties like Puff the Magic Dragon are designed to be earworms. Like the advertising jingles they resemble, they are a form of cacomagic (poopy magic) to program you to be a good consumer. You might try combatting them by replacement: put J.S. Bach's fugues on in the background when you meditate or some other complex, cerebral, instrumental music that you actually like -- I'll bet the cacomusic will have a difficult time penetrating your astral walls.

Of course I wrote an essay on this topic! https://kimberlysteele.dreamwidth.org/104928.html

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia and meditation
replies.content:
Howdy,

Two things, if I may:

1) On distraction: one of the best pieces of advice I got when I was new to meditation is that every time you notice you're distracted, you're practicing the very goal of meditation - that's the goal, not the distraction-free state, in the same way that the goal of lifting weights is for it to be a little hard, not easy. That and the idea that if you succeed in sitting there for the time you said you would, you've already accomplished won: if nothing else, you relaxed and paid attention to your breathing for a bit, which is good for you, even without the flashier mental stuff.

2) On music: when I was a teenager, I stumbled onto a form of meditation that I would now characterize as focusing on certain astral senses. I would pick a piece of instrumental music I knew and liked well that was especially beautiful (Beethoven's rendition of the "Ode to Joy" from his 9th symphony was a favorite) and focus on trying to "hear" it in my mind's ear. I noticed that this was a different feeling from trying to "make" the music in my head, as if I were humming along with my internal monologue. If you find music especially compelling, this might be a way to turn that into a target of concentration and consciousness-shifting, rather than a distraction.

Cheers, and good luck,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia and meditation
replies.content:
Adding a bit to Jeff's good advice here. In addition to 'hearing' the music as Jeff describes, I find it helpful for training my meditation-stamina to focus on other non-physical sensory meditation exercises. One of my favorites is going through a list of scents and developing them solely in my imagination: the scent of mint, of cut grass, of various foods, of the soap I use, subtler scents like that of the sea, turned earth, the milky smell of a nursing pet animal, etc. And I do the same with taste-imagination. I find that using this sensory imagery helps to build stamina for more cerebral discursive meditation.

Another suggestion: the White Chestnut Bach flower essence works really well when wanting to shed earworms and stuck thoughts or tunes; it's a useful aid for meditation or for simply clearing one's thoughts. You can drink it, or you can put it on your skin, especially on your forehead. Even better, use it to trace any kind of esoteric symbol on your forehead that will help you concentrate.

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Schizophrenia and meditation
replies.content:
Becoming conscious of your breathing is one of the best ways for most people to get into a meditative state. Allowing inhaling and exhaling to occur throughout your body tends to be the easiest way to relax chronic tension patterns that have become so habitual you no can longer perceive them. However, it doesn't work for everybody all the time. So, you may simply be experiencing the learning curve of beginning to meditate, or you may be discovering that breath focus doesn't work for you right now. If the latter is the case, then focus on something else. The goal is to train your mind to be able to maintain its focus on whatever you have chosen, breathing or otherwise.

Given that some part of you keeps offering up Puff, why not try that as the focus for your attention? If that song ceases the moment you decide to pay attention to it, perhaps even distracting you with acute awareness of your breathing, then you'll know that you're just experiencing the natural learning curve of your mind offering up endless distractions. If, however, Puff doesn't go up in a puff of smoke, there's probably something in that song that some part of you wants you to pay attention to. Would that be the lyrics, the tune, the cultural context, an important event you experienced while that song was playing, or something even more esoteric? Who knows? As with a dream, some part of you has decided that the image of that song is the best metaphor it can come up with for something it would like you to become aware of. Wouldn’t that be worth meditating on for a spell? If you’re lucky, once you’ve finished unpacking it, Puff won’t get replaced by the Barney song, but, hey, the soul always goes whither it will.

— Christophe

comments.title: The Invisible Hand
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG and all!

First, let me say how very much I enjoy your Weird of Hali books: I've reread them several times and will no doubt continue to do so over the years!

I much enjoyed this week’s Ecosophian essay on “The Neckless Ones,” and want to offer a timely example of the role of the life force. I recently developed a hand tremor, and I’ve been observing it with curiosity as well as apprehension.

There are apparently a number of ways that hand tremor can present, and among the most common is what I have, called ‘intentional tremor.’ When I reach my hand out to touch something it begins to tremble just before I actually touch the object or when I try to touch it lightly, for example when I point toward or reach toward something, trace a line of text with my finger, or lightly rest my fingers on the keyboard as I think about what to write. When my hands are fully resting, or grasping something firmly, there is less or no tremor: it’s the in-between state, the state of active intention but not yet execution, where the tremor shows itself.

This in-between state corresponds to the life force that you write about so clearly in your books on magic and occultism, JMG: the time-space that bridges between thought / intention and physical action. This, it seems to me, is where my tremor is actually occurring.

I’ve been giving this a lot of thought and exploration, seeking to understand it in an occult context as well as a physical / medical one. (I’m also pursuing medical exams and diagnosis, though probably not conventional medical treatment; tremor is not the only neurological symptom I’m experiencing, but it’s the one I want to discuss here.) The tremor started out pretty subtly about a month ago in one hand, then gained momentum and became more intense and palsy-like, and spread to my other hand as well. It was becoming quite hard to handle, so to speak. Last week I decided to undertake a course of acupuncture to see whether that made any difference; I assumed that if it did help, it would take a number of treatments before I saw any results.

After my first treatment the improvement was startling; I’ve now had two treatments and the tremor is back to the milder form it took when it began a month ago. (It would have taken me a lot of struggle to type this if it hadn’t subsided substantially.) The tremor may disappear entirely as I continue treatments; we’ll see. Interestingly, and more food for thought, is that my other neurological symptoms have not (yet) improved; it’s the ‘intentional tremor’ that has been improved through a treatment modality that affects the life force.

I figured this would be an interesting observation to share with our group here!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: The Invisible Hand
replies.content:
1) Delighted to hear this.

2) And I'm even more delighted to hear this. It makes perfect sense, as the life force is precisely what bridges the gap between mental intention and the physical body.

replies.title: Re: The Invisible Hand
replies.content:
One theory about intention tremors is a cybernetic one. Since the tremors occur with intentional action, the idea is that they involve a over-compensation in the feedback mechanism that helps one close in on the target (and then perhaps rigidity when trying to block the tremor). I'm glad that acupuncture seems to be helping; it's the kind of problem acupuncture should be good for.

You might also try a few experiments that might indicate some workarounds. One would be acting without intention, or without a specific target. Another would be entraining the etheric mechanism to an astral one, by carefully visualizing a precise, particular movement that would ordinarily entail a tremor, and then dropping the visualization and performing the movement spontaneously, without thinking (intending) it. A third would be using a rhythmic, musical pattern to support the movement -- say, by listening to some well-structured, equable piece of music and then letting the movement "ride" on the music. (I suppose this last is also a matter of taste: some people might find Mozart helpful, and others Megadeth!)

The basic rationale is that etheric processes are "structuring energy" -- from one point of view emerging properties of systems, from another point of view the energy patterns that lead or attract system processes. Rhythm is more etheric, but adding imagery and emotion entrain the astral, which supports and reshapes the etheric.

Best wishes!

LeGrand

FWIW, I do think long hair acts as an antenna of sorts, though I cannot pin down the exact mechanics of it. What I do know is that I have not cut my hair in probably twenty years, and some years ago, I started wearing it up and covering with a bandana, whenever I leave the house. Keeps it from getting snagged in car doors and mussed by the wind. Previously I had either worn it loose (and a bit messy) or in a bun.

A remarkable thing happened with that change: there was a noticeable shift in how strangers in public places behave toward me. It is hard to describe, but I get less social friction. Some of that may simply be a class signaling thing: long messy natural hair is low class where I live. Not showing my hair at all removes that from the constellation of signs that strangers use to "place" me. With less info, they're both nicer and less interested.

But the piece of that I can't really account for is: I used to be a *magnet* for crazy people. Like, at the park, or just pushing my cart through the grocery store minding my own business, or whatever, if there was a crazy person anywhere in the vicinity, they would find me, and start an alarming conversation that I would then have to find a way to scarper from. Men, women, didn't matter. There I'd be looking for the right yogurt, and here some woman I'd never met was launching into a diatribe about The Jews and the Illuminati, loudly, like I was a good friend from the bowling league or something. And that NEVER happens anymore-- not once-- since I took up the bandana fashion. I'm flummoxed. But it's got something to do with the hair!

comments.title: Again with the "Apropos of Nothing" ?!?
comments.content:
Sorry to wool gather on here, but perhaps some feedback would help clarify my thoughts. Feel free to skip.
I have been spending a bunch of time reading "Hellenic Tantra" by Gregory Shaw, who is a scholar of neoplatonic philosophy and an expert on Iamblichus. He is proposing that the study of neo-Platonic philosophers has been distorted by the dualist mindset of, well, everybody in academe really, and that Iamblichus and Theurgy can only be understood from the understanding that Iamblichus was a non-dualist. He also points out interestingly (and I had never actually realized this) that Iamblichus was engaged in a polemic with the dualist thinking of Plotinus, as explicated by Plotinus' amenuensis Porphyry, and that Iamblichus WON that argument, since all the later exponents of Plato's thinking were theurgists, engaging in rituals using symbolic objects in which the essence of the Gods were ACTUALLY embodied as means to bring their consciousnesses into congruence with that of the Gods.

I find this extremely interesting as a practitioner, but the problem that I have is with the insistence that the non-dualist concept means that we are inextricably involved with physical embodiment. His idea is that enlightenment exists in the body, that embodiement in the physical universe is demiurgey (engaging in the creative process of the demiurge. But I think any assertions along this line fall into the trap we face when we, as denizens of the physical, finite world, with bodies that live and die, try to talk comprehensively about anything that is infinite and eternal. All we have is analogies, and a good analogy is good because of how far it goes before it breaks down, but break down it will, especially when the topic is eternity.

But anyway, I suspect that speculating about all this is useful for building the "mental body" (as this analogical system would put it).

THanks for your patience. I have too much time on my hands and too many words in my head...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do bacteria and fungi have consciousness? If they do, then at what stage do they become plants or animals?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The material I've read suggests that they're on a different evolutionary track and are not part of the line that leads to plants and animals. What their long-term destiny is hasn't been discussed in any work available to me.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's no where near as simple as fungi transitioning to plants transitioning to animals. While it may be humbling for us as language-wielding animals to consider that we may not be the apogee of incarnate existence, we're really not. Heck, we're not even the most advanced of the language-wielding, vertebrate animals, although we are plenty close to that title... so long as we’re only considering this planet.

As for fungi and plants, they have their own trajectories that include attaining full sentience within their own categories. The greatest of the fungi networks, the larger mycelial mats that grow their own forests and control the local weather, appear to be vastly more intelligent than we humans. However, we won't ever graduate to become them, as we are simply on different developmental paths.

Even within the category of animals there are divergent paths with no overlap. Despite the great, globe-straddling ant-colonies being many orders of magnitude beyond any human's ability to affect its surroundings, we won't ever graduate to become some hive-colony intelligence either. The exoskeletal species are on a distinct path, such that some mosquito we kill may come back as a fly, followed by a crab, then a small wasp colony, and eventually as a beehive.

The communal hive intelligence experiment is distinct from the fungal film, slime, and mat intelligence experiment. Both of those are distinct from the independent endoskeletal intelligence experiment we are part of. We happen to live on a very creative planet, and she's running all kinds of wild experiments in tandem. Here, I've only begun to touch on a few of the ones we can even hope to conceive of. What about all the super-slow ones we wouldn't be able to recognize, like swamps becoming ponds becoming streams becoming rivers, or stones coming back as hills coming back as mountains coming back as tectonic plates? Or dare we even consider how our gut-biome communities have far-reaching control over our mood, health, and behavior? Now, who would be the most intelligent and powerful in that essential relationship?

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In my understanding, microbial beings have group-consciousness rather than individual. An interesting exploration of this is the book "Microbes and Other Shamanic Beings" by Amerindian anthropologist and ethnographer Cesar Giraldo Herrera. He argues that a shamanic belief-system using the idea of 'evil spirits' causing illnesses, as reported by early missionaries to Central and South American peoples, was a Christian distortion of understanding, based on Christian ideas of evil spirits. Instead, he argues, the old shamans had more of a modern scientific understanding of physical microbial populations, but that they understood these microbial species to have collective consciousnesses and addressed their shamanic practices accordingly.

Fungi are amazing beings with enormous, interconnected underground mycelial systems that link together trees and plants into communication networks. Merlin Sheldrake's wonderful book "Entangled Life: How Fungi Make our World" is a very fine introduction to these beings and their world, illustrated by the author using ink he made from mushrooms.

I don't know how these beings move along the stages of spiritual evolution, but it seems to me that a deeper understanding of their intricate, collective physical and metaphysical lives is necessary in order to seek answers to these questions.

Winifred

replies.title: Re: the mind's eye
replies.content:
Thinking about unpleasantly killing those who have offended you is called cursing. It might fit into the category of little white curses when you think "I hope you crash and burn, Jackass!" after someone cuts you off in traffic. You didn't put much intention or imagination into it, and they will be able to completely shed your malignity after a good meal, a workout, or a short nap. Guess where that malignity is most likely to go after they shed it off. Yeah, just like a homing pigeon!

A well-visualized and detailed rehearsal of enjoying murdering an imagined adversary is more like voodoo-doll lite. You didn't act out any spiteful physical rituals, but you still meticulously built up a spiteful charge. They may be able to clear your hostility after a full night's recovery, or it may be sufficient to get stuck to them for the rest of their life. If that curse is not able to come flying back like a homing pigeon, it will get added to your karma to send your next life spiraling off-balance. Practice something else, instead.

You've clearly got a gift, so what would you like to do with it? How about figuring out who cursed you as a teen to get you stuck in that nasty habit? Someone modeled that indulgent behavior for you (and probably at you), leaving you painfully vulnerable to its self-soothing allure. That could have happened in a previous life, making this the karma you have to work off this time round, but I doubt that's the case. With such a potent gift, I imagine you were terrifyingly threatening in your youth to plenty of people, so they willed curses and deathwishes at you in their terror. You then got stuck with their fear and insecurity and bitterness. You can get unstuck just as easily, probably more easily in fact, because that habit of theirs is not in your true nature.

Some part of you that has not been fully nurtured and appreciated is resorting to self-soothing behavior, even though it's also self-destructive. Discover how to nurture and appreciate that creative, imaginal part of yourself, and watch how quickly you begin dwelling on nurturing and appreciative imagery instead. Thoughts about unpleasantly killing can then naturally untwist out of their contorted state to become thoughts about pleasantly blessing. As usual, our greatest weaknesses are just our greatest gifts twisted all out of shape.

— Christophe

Sometimes it is scary to see so many parallels.  Perhaps this question is too broad, but what do you see as the main tools in magic for healing polarization and projection?

replies.title: Sailing

I've recently discovered a website with a comprehensive analysis of Greek mythology. Poseidon rules the subconscious (represented by the sea). Boats and sailing figure prominently in quests. From the website:

"Minos ‘”he purification of discerning intelligence” had a slave named Naucrate “who dominates on sea” (self-control). Here the sea (nau) is not a symbol of the vital, but of sailing, i.e. the evolutionary process of the quest.
Another interpretation would be based on Naucrate as a “vital power” or “power over the vital”. replies.content:
0) Good to hear from you!

1) Thanks for the endorsement of The Druid Path - So far, I've skipped it for similar reasons, but sounds like I might find it valuable nonetheless.

2) I don't have a source, but sailors are famously superstitious, so a collection of such beliefs might have some interesting interpretations for the occult minded.

3) The first sailing-relevant fiction that comes to mind is Patrick O'Brien's Aubrey-Maturin books (made famous by the film Master and Commander, starring Russell Crowe and Paul Bettany as Aubrey and Maturin). It's more military/sailing-technical fiction than spiritual, but there might some relevant themes in there, as Aubrey's Anglican religiosity is at times pitted against Maturin's scientific worldview (they have kind of a Kirk-Bones thing going on). Next, Moby Dick popped into my head, which I haven't read, but which certainly has psychological/religious themes and by all accounts lots of sailing detail. As for non-fiction, A.T. Mahan's The Influence of Sea Power Upon History doesn't so much go into the nitty-gritty of sailing, but talks about how naval power has shaped cultures and their impact on the world (one of my favorite examples, as something I should have known but didn't: in the middle phases of the struggle for imperial dominance between England and France, France prioritized disrupting England's naval success in the Americas, and England focused on defeating France in the Indies. This resulted in both the success of the American Revolution and England's role as the dominant European power in the subcontinent). I read it before getting much into our host's stuff, but now that I reflect, it strikes me that it might have some interesting perspectives on a Spenglerian view of history, so possibly relevant depending on how personally/widely you want to focus.

Cheers,
Jeff

Additionally there may be and probably are "abstract" causes, by which I mean causes on a different plane than the physical. Can these "root-causes" and their connections into the present be seen (or used) as pathways for "things" like synchronicity, correspondence and magic to work along?

I instantly felt negativity and pressure being taken away from me so I gather it worked - but I wanted to ask anyway, ...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello John Mike Greer. Thank you hosting another magic monday. A lot of interesting questions as always.

I do wish to ask you though. What are your opinions on magicians taking antidepressants even when they are regular in their daily practice? Would it seem counterintuitive to numb the brain when they’re trying to develop their occult powers? Or would you say that one can make use of the occult rituals to overcome the problems that caused the need for those anti-depressants?

What do you think of fasting besides meditation and daily practice?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I am not a doctor, first of all. Nothing I say should be construed as medical advice.

I would encourage you to do some research into the antidepressant you are taking. A great deal of recent evidence, including controlled double-blind studies, suggests that many antidepressants do no good and a lot of harm. You might consider seeing if you can find a physician who is, as the phrase is, "a healer rather than a dealer," who will taper you safely off the one you are on.

All of which is to say that they do not help your magical practice, at all, and many people find that they also don't help their psychological state. Again, though, I am not a doctor; please consult with a competent medical practitioner.

2) Some people thrive on regular fasting. This guy --
[IMAGE:0]
--Joseph Greenstein, the last of the old-time strongmen, used to say that fasting one day a week was an essential part of his training regimen. Since he could still tie an iron horseshoe in an overhand knot with his bare hands at the age of 70, he may have had a clue... ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For a decidedly non-occult look at fasting and its potential benefits, you might check out Dr. Peter Attia (your favorite web search will turn up lots of hits, but some of his interviews with Tim Ferriss are good intros if you like podcasts). I haven't checked in on what he's doing lately, but last I heard, he practiced something like time-restricted eating most days (basically skipping breakfast and sometimes lunch as well), fasting one day a week, and then once a quarter or so a longer fast of a few days. Obviously, check with a doctor before trying anything out, and definitely don't try to go from zero to sixty (if you've never fasted before, don't try to fast for a week or something crazy).

My own experience was before getting into occultism, based on his and some others' advice about the ketogenic diet (very short take: it seems to be amazing for some folks, okay for others, and terrible for a few). Basically, when you fast for an extended period, your body burns up all the sugar it has stored and starts converting fat into ketones, because regular fat can't get into your brain for energy. The ketogenic diet is meant to get this process going while still eating, but it's easiest to kickstart by fasting. Tim Ferriss's book Tools for Titans has some tips here, if I remember right.

If you decide to give it a go, a few tips: drink lots of water, and put a bit of salt in that water (just a pinch, you don't need to taste it). Many folks ease into fasting with a broth fast (you drink a small amount of broth during the fasting period) rather than going straight to a water fast (you can only drink water and maybe black coffee or tea). Don't do any intense exercise, but going for a long, leisurely walk early in the process might help. Get plenty of sleep. Avoid extreme temperatures, especially heat.

As I said, none of that gets into the magical/spiritual side of things, but it's certainly an exercise of will, especially near the beginning when you haven't gotten used to it yet.

Anyhow, hope some of this helps,
Jeff

comments.title: Evolution of the Bodies
comments.content:
IIRC, you have said that the etheric body is born (in humans) at around age 7, the astral at around age 14, and the mental sheath at around age 21.

Until recently, I assumed (because now I realize I never really thought about it) that since that is how the bodies are 'born', that is how they evolve.

But I recently read an interpretation that has the bodies forming around the divine spark in a different order during involution and I'm curious if the order of appearance of the bodies you describe aligns with their actual involution or am I just imposing a materialistic ontogony-recapitulates-phylogony understanding on something that I shouldn't.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Evolution of the Bodies
replies.content: The birth of each body is its separation from the matrix in which it is formed and the beginning of its independent life. Think of material birth: the material body of the infant stops being inclosed by, and dependent on, the mother's body and begins to breathe, move, and digest independently. The etheric body separates from the etheric matrix of the family around age seven, the astral body separates from the astral matrix of the community around age fourteen, and the mental sheath detaches from the raw substance of the mental plane around age 21. All of them have a long process of involutionary development before then, and it happens in the opposite order -- mental, then astral, then etheric, and then material.

replies.title: Re: Evolution of the Bodies
replies.content: That helps tremendously.  Thank you so much.

replies.title: Re: Evolution of the Bodies
replies.content:
If I may ask a follow up: you've talked a bit about what these separations look like practically: material separation is what you just described here, etheric would presumably be getting more energy from independent activity than from the family, and astral developing independent thoughts and feelings rather than "borrowing" them from the community. What then does mental separation mean for the individual? The beginning of true insight into meaning, values, and patterns, rather than taking them on faith/as logical constructs? Or is it more your own insights into these things rather than from some wider source?

Thanks much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Evolution of the Bodies
replies.content: The birth of the mental sheath is the point at which it becomes possible to have personal perceptions of meaning, value, and intention, rather than simply following along with their astral equivalents, which normally come from your family and culture.

" For Osiris On-Nophris who is found perfect before the Gods, hath said:

Perfected through Suffering, Glorified through Trial.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

What are your thoughts on whether we humans have free will? Is it possible that we have no/limited free will in the everyday mundane sense but that free will operates at a less obvious level, or within a larger sphere of influence beyond our current comprehension? How do you view the interface of free will and the belief in degrees of reward and punishment/suffering the ("between life") Beyond? I believe Einstein rejected an afterlife on the basis of the seeming incompatibility of determinism and reward/suffering.

Many Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Human beings are not born with free will but they are capable of achieving it. At first it's little more than a bit of wiggle room here and there, but it can be extended through sustained effort and reflective self-awareness. The whole business about experiences between lives has been massively misunderstood by people who had serious daddy issues; it's not about getting patted on the head if you were good little children or spanked if you were naughty.

What happens during the afterlife is that you get to experience your own life objectively with all its consequences. That's a very pleasant thing to go through if you used your life well and the opposite if you wasted it on selfishness, cruelty, or what have you. You also go through this experience at whatever level of the astral plane you resonated with at the time of your death -- and the astral plane has some regions that are pretty close to heaven and some that make a very good version of Hell. But it's not a matter of rewards and punishments, just of seeing what actually happened without any hope of evasion or self-deception.

Good evening!

A stupid question: how often could discursive meditation be practiced? An obvious answer would be "as often as possible", but I have a few considerations on the contrary:

1) Unity of will. It feels intuitive that one should finish a series of meditations on a certain subject (a book, for example), instead of juggling unrelated or semi-related subjects. This could lead to some bottlenecks.

2) Personal experience. I often end up blanking when meditating on a subject, and then have insights suddenly arriving hours later, as if I'm planting a seed into my subconscious and it needs some time to sprout. Maybe I'm doing something wrong.

3) I think either Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose or Modern Essene Fellowship papers mention diminishing returns when it comes to more than three meditation sessions a day, but they are dealing with a different kind of meditation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Once a day, for no more than fifteen minutes at first, working up to a maximum of thirty minutes. More than that and you start getting mentally stale.

In the given example in the book, the magician takes on an imaginary form of the animal and goes about the neighborhood in a sensorial fashion. But what changes can be expected from these animal forms?

The cat and the dog are the most obvious forms that come to mind. Since the book lists animals with zodiacal, planetary, and elemental associations, would the temporary changes experienced align with the general effects of ritual work corresponding to those signs, planets, and elements?

One example given was of photographic memory. Another example was to heighten abilities for physical combat. Do we give the charge to the animal form to ‘develop photographic memory’ or will this happen in tandem with all other abilities of the animal? In our chosen e.g, the elephant has the best memory of all. Elephant - fire, Jupiter, Taurus.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Next time you have very complex questions, please ask them earlier in the day. You're going to get briefer and potentially grumpier answers this close to closing.

1) That's a personal question and each practitioner will answer it differently.

2a) The traditional teachings are what they are. Sure, you can find apparent contradictions in them, and that's useful if it leads you to meditate on them and figure out what unity of will is veiled behind them. Saying "but this doesn't make sense!" without going on to do the meditations isn't going to get you anywhere, though.

2b) The distinction between celestial and terrestrial isn't especially useful, since many magical rituals -- including these -- have elements of both.

2c) Yes, the temporary changes are generally aligned with the symbolism, but they will also be specific to the animal in question.

2d) You will get all the characteristics of the animal. For example, if you take on an elephant's body of transformation, your memory may well become more retentive, but so will your body's fat cells. This is why I specified temporary expansion of human capacities, and advised against doing repeated workings of the same animal because of the lasting effect on your body, emotions, and mind.
